title: Chapter 39: The After
content: One year later.

The lights came up on the runway, and the audience rose before the last model had cleared the stage. Not the polite, folding-chair shuffle of Parisian convention — this was a wave. Bodies pressing forward, hands clapping above heads, throats open. Bertha Mason stood at the end of the catwalk in her punk tuxedo — the same one, restructured, the seams now visible by design, the lapels sliced into angles that defied geometry — and she did not cry.
She had done enough of that.

MASON ATELIER glowed in red thread above the entrance, and beneath the lettering, smaller but impossible to miss: the HELP code. Four letters stitched in scarlet cotton into the breast of every piece in the collection. Not a logo. A declaration. A woman's voice pressed into fabric because no other channel would carry it, and now worn on the chests of models walking the most watched runway in the world.

The reviews called it fearless. Bertha called it Tuesday. The air in the Grand Palais had been thick with perfume and anticipation, and when she took her bow the ovation rolled toward her like weather, and she stood in the sound of it and let it wash over her without flinching. The punk tuxedo held its shape on her shoulders — restructured, yes, but the same garment, the same seams she had sewn in a room without windows, now walking in daylight.

She had chosen her own doctors — a psychiatrist in the Marais who listened before prescribing, a therapist who understood that management did not mean erasure. Lithium, lamotrigine, weekly sessions. The lows still came. The highs still glowed dangerous. But they were hers now, charted and anticipated, not a prison sentence disguised as mercy. She'd fired the first three doctors Edward's people had found before she found her own. The fourth stayed.

The punk tuxedo entered the permanent collection of the Musée des Arts Décoratifs three months after the show. The placard read: *Punk Tuxedo (2025), Bertha Mason. Cotton, silk, red thread. Gift of the artist.* A woman in the archive whispered that the HELP stitches were barely visible unless you knew where to look. Bertha had designed it that way. You had to lean in. You had to choose to see.

Helen Burns won the Pulitzer Prize for Investigative Journalism on a Monday in April. The announcement came over the wire at noon, and by twelve-fifteen her inbox held four thousand emails and her phone had melted into a decorative brick. She let it ring.

The Thornfield series — fourteen parts, two million words of testimony and document and forensic accounting traced through shell companies and forged signatures — had become a book. *The Thread.* Hardcover, no photographs on the cover, just red stitching on cream paper. It sat on the bestseller list for thirty-seven weeks. Translated into twenty-two languages. Optioned for a limited series by a studio that promised, in writing, to hire Helen as consulting producer.

Her editor — the one who'd told her to soften the third installment, who'd suggested that maybe Edward Rochester deserved nuance — retired early. Sixty-three, sudden, a press release about spending time with family. Helen sent flowers. White lilies. No card.

She did not gloat. That was the thing about Helen. She could have. She had earned every right to dance on the rubble of the man who'd tried to bury the story, and instead she sent lilies and moved on to the next investigation — a textile conglomerate in Bangladesh with supply chains that read like horror fiction. Same thread, different spool. Helen's desk was never clear. She liked it that way. Stacks of manila folders and printed emails and court filings, the raw material of accountability. She slept six hours a night and drank her coffee black and never once considered writing a memoir. The work was the memoir.

The Pulitzer ceremony required a speech. Helen spoke for ninety seconds. She thanked her sources. She named them. Bertha Mason, Grace Poole, Adèle Varens, Jane Eyre. She said, "A journalist is only as good as the people who trust her with the truth," and sat down. The audience filled the silence with applause so sustained the orchestra had to wait.
Blanche Ingram launched a podcast.

This was, by any metric, an unmappable pivot from the woman who had spent a decade curating her image like a Fabergé egg — glossed, sealed, displayed at the correct angle. But Blanche had watched Thornfield implode from the inside, had felt the walls buckle, and something in her had cracked open instead of closed. She stopped performing perfection and started performing honesty, which turned out to be the better show.

*Unraveling.* That was the name. Weekly interviews with women in fashion — designers, seamstresses, models, buyers, the invisible hands behind the visible seams. Her tagline, delivered in that low, smoky voice that had once sold perfume and now sold truth: *Every stitch tells a story. We're listening now.*

The first episode — an interview with a former intern who'd been silenced by an NDA — hit two million downloads in a week. By the sixth month, Blanche had a studio, a producer, and a waiting list of women who wanted to talk. She still wore black. Always black. But it was her black now — chosen, not mourned. She'd stopped being a billboard for other people's aesthetics and started being a signal fire for other people's stories.

Blanche never interviewed Bertha. That was the one question every journalist asked, and she always said no. "Some stories aren't mine to narrate," she told a reporter from *Le Monde.* "I just make room for them." The reporter wrote that Blanche Ingram had found something more valuable than beauty: purpose.
Blanche read the piece over coffee, wearing a black silk shirt she'd bought at a vintage shop in Montmartre, and thought: *Close enough.*

Adèle Varens turned twenty in October, and she spent her birthday in the textiles lab at the École supérieure des arts et industries textiles in Roubaix, running tensile strength tests on a biodegradable polymer blend she'd developed from mushroom mycelium and recycled cotton. There was a smudge of compound on her left cheek and a coffee stain on her lab coat and she had never looked more like herself.

She chose textile engineering because of a diary — Jane's mother's diary, the one discovered in the Thornfield archive, pages of careful script describing thread counts and dye processes and the way fabric moved against skin. Adèle had read it in one sitting, cross-legged on Jane's floor in Paris, and something had clicked. Not the romance of fashion, not the glamour, but the mechanics. The science of how things held together. How supply chains could be rebuilt. How the industry that had chewed up women for a century might be rewoven from the ground up.

She wanted genuinely sustainable supply chains. Not the greenwashed kind — the real kind. Traceable fibers, fair wages, closed-loop water systems. She was twenty years old and she had already presented at two conferences and turned down three corporate sponsorships that came with strings she could see from a mile away.
Jane had taught her that. How to spot the strings. How to refuse them.

Adèle called Jane every Sunday. They talked about lab reports and bad dates and whether the mushroom polymer was ever going to achieve the drape of silk. (It wasn't. Not yet. She was working on it.) Sometimes they didn't talk at all — just sat on the phone together, breathing, existing in the same frequency across three hundred kilometers.

She was brave in the way that young women are brave when they've already survived the worst and discovered they're still standing. Not fearless. Brave. The kind that wakes up every morning and chooses the work anyway.

Grace Poole received immunity in exchange for her testimony. The prosecution had wanted more — wanted her to carry more weight, more blame — but Helen's documentation and Bertha's corroborating account made it clear: Grace had been a guard and a prisoner in the same breath. The court recognized what Edward had always refused to: that keeping a woman in a locked room required someone else to be locked in there with her.

She moved to Lyon. Took a position at a women's shelter — security consultant, weekend overnight shifts. The work was different. The skills were the same: reading a room, recognizing escalation, knowing which doors locked and which ones opened. But here the locks kept danger out instead of in, and the women behind them opened their own doors when they were ready.

Grace was not good at tenderness. She was not warm. The shelter director had hired her anyway, said, "I don't need warm. I need safe." Grace had nodded once and started that night. The women at the shelter did not always understand her at first — she was tall and watchful and she moved through the hallways with the economy of someone who had spent years anticipating the next crisis. But they learned. They learned that when Grace said the perimeter was secure, it was. They learned that she never asked about their pasts, never pressed for details, never made them explain. She just made sure the doors held.

Sometimes, walking the perimeter at three in the morning, she saw red thread. In a scarf left on a chair, in a spool in the craft room where residents made small things to steady their hands. Red thread, winding through. She thought of the scissors — the ones Bertha had held, the ones that cut through twenty years of silence in a single night — and she did not flinch. She checked the locks. She walked the hall. The shelter stayed safe.

She never contacted Bertha. But once, in a hardware store, she saw a bolt of red cotton thread and stood in the aisle for a long time before walking away without buying it. Some things you don't own. Some things you just recognize.
Jane Eyre went back to TikTok.

This was not a comeback. There had been no departure, no dramatic exit, no farewell tour. She had simply stopped posting for three months while Thornfield burned and the legal depositions piled up and the world decided what to make of her. Then one afternoon she sat on the floor of her Paris apartment in a vintage Depeche Mode T-shirt and cargo pants, pressed record, and said: "So. A lot of you have questions."

Forty-seven million people watched that video. It was not the one about the trial, not the one about the testimony, not the one where she read Edward's contract aloud and paused at the non-disclosure clause long enough for the silence to become a verdict. The one that broke the internet was simpler.

A journalist — print, male, condescending — had asked her on a red carpet whether she regretted destroying Thornfield. Whether she felt responsible for the fallout, the layoffs, the collapse of a fashion house that had employed hundreds. Jane had looked at him with the expression she reserved for people who confused consequences with fault and said:
"I didn't destroy Thornfield. Edward did. I just held up a mirror."

The clip ran on every platform. It became a T-shirt, a tattoo, a rally cry. Jane did none of those things. She posted it once and moved on to a video about the cut of a vintage Yves Saint Laurent blazer, because she had said what she needed to say and repeating it would have turned conviction into branding.

She was writing a book. Not *The Thread* — Helen's book, Helen's territory. This one was about fabric and power and the women who built the industry with their hands and their silence and their red thread. She wrote in the mornings, longhand, on a desk by a window that overlooked a courtyard where someone hung laundry in the afternoons. The sight of it — sheets and shirts and the occasional red scarf pinned to a line — steadied her.

She was not engaged. She was not seeing anyone. She was not in therapy about it, not journaling about it, not working toward it. She was wearing what she wanted, eating when she was hungry, sleeping when she was tired, and posting videos that cut through the noise of the internet like a pair of scissors through silk.
Just Jane.

The world wanted a redemption arc. The world wanted a love interest, a glow-up, a woman reshaped by suffering into something palatable and pretty and easy to swallow. The world wanted Bertha medicated into flatness and Helen softened and Blanche humbled and Adèle grateful and Grace forgiven and Jane — Jane most of all — wanted in a way that made sense to people who had never held a pair of scissors in a corridor at midnight.
The world did not get what it wanted.

These women got what they built. With their hands and their stitch-rippers and their testimony and their refusal to be the versions of themselves that Thornfield had required. Not because the system was kind — the system had buried Bertha for twenty years and would have buried her for twenty more. Not because justice was swift — it took nine months of court proceedings and four thousand pages of evidence to prove what a single stitch had always known.

Everyone found their way after because they were stronger than the architecture that had tried to contain them. Because red thread holds. Because a woman who cannot speak will find another way to say what she means, and the women who hear her will not look away.
Not because the system was kind.
Because these women were stronger.
